content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 16, 2001


Sarah Gilmer
Olson, Hagel, Waters
& Fishburn, LLP
Plaza Towers
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 1425
Sacramento, CA 95814-4602

Re: Advice Letter No. A-01-012

Dear Ms. Gilmer:

Pursuant to our discussion of this morning, please find enclosed a corrected copy
of the Bustamante Advice Letter, No. A-01-012. You stated that you are representing
Lieutenant Governor Cruz M. Bustamante in connection with the advice request.

As we discussed, the conclusion of the original letter contained a typographical
error that was inconsistent with the analysis in the letter. This error has been corrected.
We are herein reissuing the letter to you on Mr. Bustamante's behalf. Please discard the
prior version of the letter. We apologize for any inconvenience the error may have
caused.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


January 23, 2001


Cruz M. Bustamante, Lieutenant Governor
State Capitol, Room 1114
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-01-012

Dear Lieut. Governor Bustamante:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the "mass-mailing" prohibition in the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

1. May you send 200 of each of the following in a calendar month consistent with the provisions of section 89001 and regulation 18901 which concern the mass mailing prohibition:

• A letter congratulating an Eagle Scout;

• A letter congratulating a restaurant owner, commending him for having his restaurant named as one of the best in California;

• A letter congratulating a person on his or her birthday;

• A letter congratulating an individual on being a Rhodes Scholar;

• A letter congratulating individuals on contributions made in public service?


___________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91015. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18996, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-01-012
Page No. 2

2. If a commendation and resolution are placed in an envelope together, would
they be considered as two substantially similar items?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Each item within the specific category is substantially similar to those in that
category. However, each category may be treated as a separate mass mailing.

2. No. The commendation is substantially similar to commendations for the
same or similar events. The cover letter will be substantially similar to other cover letters
only if one of the definitions of "substantially similar" in regulation 18901 apply. Each
set of "substantially similar" items is limited to 200 in a calendar month when combined
with other "substantially similar" items.

FACTS

You requested assistance concerning congratulatory and commendatory
documents (examples were attached to your letter.) You asked whether all
congratulatory letters are treated as "substantially similar" items. For example, you
asked whether (1) a letter congratulating an Eagle Scout on his accomplishments is
considered "substantially similar" to (2) a letter to a restaurant owner commending him
for having his restaurant named as one of the best in California, or whether (3) a letter
congratulating a person on his or her birthday is substantially similar to (4) a letter
congratulating an individual on being a Rhodes Scholar, or (5) a letter congratulating
individuals on contributions made in public service.

ANALYSIS

Section 89001 of the Act provides: "No newsletter or other mass mailing shall be
sent at public expense." Regulation 18901 clarifies the scope of this broad prohibition.
Pursuant to regulation 18901(a), a mass mailing is prohibited only if four criteria are met:

"(1) Any item sent is delivered, by any means, to the
recipient at his or her residence, place of employment or business
or post office box. For purposes of this subdivision (a)(1), the item
delivered to the recipient must be a tangible item, such as a
videotape, record, or button, or a written document.

"(2) The item sent either:

"(A) Features an elected officer affiliated with the agency
which produces or sends the mailing, or

File No. A-01-012
Page No. 3

"(B) Includes the name, office, photograph, or other
reference to an elected officer affiliated with the agency which
produces or sends the mailing, and is prepared or sent in
cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the elected
officer.

"(3)(A) Any of the costs of distribution is paid for with
public moneys; or

"(B) Costs of design, production, and printing exceeding
$50.00 are paid with public moneys, and the design, production, or
printing is done with the intent of sending the item other than as
permitted by this regulation.

"(4) More than two hundred substantially similar items are
sent, in a single calendar month, excluding any item sent in
response to an unsolicited request and any item described in
subdivision (b)."

Your question concerns this last requirement. As discussed above, section 89001
applies only to a "mass mailing" as defined in the Act. Regulation 18901(a)(4) clarifies
that if less than 201 of the same or substantially similar items are sent in a calendar
month, the regulation would not restrict the mailing. Separate mailings may be counted
together if the items sent are substantially similar. Two items are "substantially similar"
if any of the following applies:

"(1) The items are identical, except for changes necessary
to identify the recipient and his or her address.

"(2) The items are intended to honor, commend,
congratulate, or recognize an individual or group, or individuals or
groups, for the same event or occasion; are intended to celebrate or
recognize the same holiday; or are intended to congratulate an
individual or group, or individuals or groups, on the same type of
event, such as birthdays or anniversaries.

"(3) Both of the following apply to the items mailed:

"(a) Most of the bills, legislation, governmental action,
activities, events, or issues of public concern mentioned in one
item are mentioned in the other.

"(b) Most of the information contained in one item is
contained in the other." (Regulation 18901(c)(3).)

File No. A-01-012
Page No. 4

Thus, if two mailings, each of less than 201, are substantially similar, they will be
counted together to determine whether more than 200 items are sent in a calendar month.

You do not appear to dispute that all birthday letters are substantially similar to
other birthday letters (or all restaurant-owner commendation letters are similar to other
restaurant-owner letters). These would clearly be considered "substantially similar"
under both the definition in subsection (1) and subsection (2) since the items in each
described group are identical, except for changes necessary to identify the recipient and
his or her address, and the items are intended to honor, commend, congratulate, or
recognize an individual or group, or individuals or groups on the same type of event.

Your question is whether the various commendation letters would be considered
substantially similar to one another such that all would be considered in determining
whether the mailing was a mass mailing. Subdivision (c) would treat these items as
substantially similar under three discrete circumstances:

• The items are intended to honor, commend, congratulate, or recognize an individual
or group, or individuals or groups, for the same event or occasion. This would not
apply to the various items you have asked about since the recipients are not all being
commended for the same event or occasion.

• The items are intended to celebrate or recognize the same holiday. This would also
not apply.

• The items are intended to congratulate an individual or group, or individuals or
groups, on the same type of event, such as birthdays or anniversaries.

Thus, while all congratulatory letters are similar, they are only "substantially
similar" if they are for "same type of event." Under your facts, each of the "events" is
different and may be considered separately.

Your office also informed us by telephone that generally these commendations
are sent after the recipient, or a person on behalf of the recipient, requests that such a
commendation be issued. Regulation 18901(c)(4) provides that an otherwise prohibited
newsletter or mass mailing is exempted from the restrictions of section 89001 and
regulation 18901 if it contains only information or material sent in response to an
unsolicited specific request. As used in this regulation, a request is only unsolicited if it
is not requested or induced by the elected officer or any other person acting at his or her
behest. (Regulation 18901(c)(4)(A).) A communication sent in response to an elected
officer's participation at a public forum or press conference, or to his or her issuance of a
press release, shall be considered an unsolicited request. (Regulation 18901(c)(4)(D).)

File No. A-01-012
Page No. 5

Assuming some of the items fall within this category, they will not be counted toward the
200 allowed under the mass mailing prohibition.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
Acting General Counsel


By:	John W. Wallace
Senior Counsel
Legal Division

Corrected: March 16, 2001

sent at public expense." Regulation 18901 clarifies the scope of this broad prohibition.

"(1) Any item sent is delivered, by any means, to the
recipient at his or her residence, place of employment or business,
or post office box. For purposes of this subdivision (a)(l), the item
delivered to the recipient must be a tangible item, such as a
videotape, record, or button, or a written document.
"(2) The item sent either:
"(A) Features an elected oflicer affiliated with the agency
which produces or sends the mailing, or
"(B) Includes the name, office, photograph, or other
reference to an elected ofiicer affrliated with the agency which
produces or sends the mailing, and is prepared or sent in
cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the elected

"(3)(A) Any of the costs of distribution is paid for with
public moneys; or
"(B) Costs of design, production, and printing exceeding
